skillName: Solve problems involving tables and timetables
misconceptionSummary: Students treat time as base-10, not understanding that 60 minutes = 1 hour.

visuals.ruleText: Time uses base 60 for minutes and seconds — NOT base 10
visuals.subText: 60 minutes = 1 hour; there is no such thing as 1:75 or 2:80

steps.narration: Unlike our decimal number system, time uses base sixty for minutes and seconds. Sixty minutes fills an hour completely — there is no seventieth minute. This is why standard subtraction breaks down with time.

lines.text: Subtract 12 from hours 13–23 to get pm

steps.narration: Timetables use twenty-four hour clock to avoid am/pm ambiguity. Hours from thirteen to twenty-three are in the afternoon and evening — subtract twelve to convert to twelve-hour time.

lines.text: Read across a row for one train's journey
Read down a column for all trains at a stop
Dash (—) means the train does not stop there

steps.narration: Timetable rows show one service's journey from start to end. Columns show all services passing through one stop. A dash means the service does not call at that stop — do not treat it as midnight or zero.

visuals.expression: 60 min = 1 h — time is base 60, not base 10
visuals.label: Count on in stages — standard subtraction fails for time

steps.narration: Time works in base sixty. Count on in stages — to the next hour, through whole hours, then to the destination — rather than subtracting digits directly.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating time arithmetic like decimal arithmetic. 60 minutes make 1 hour — you cannot subtract minutes from hours directly without converting. Count on instead.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Time is base 60 for minutes. You cannot borrow 1 hour as 10 minutes — it becomes 60. Count forward in stages to find durations accurately.
